modelDescription: An OC1-level actor (hobbyist hacker or amateur with limited professional expertise, several days of effort, and up to $1,000 budget) targets a financially attractive SME or regional bank with a phishing and Business Email Compromise (BEC) campaign. The actor scrapes employee contacts, registers a look-alike domain, sends phishing emails at scale, compromises an inbox, monitors for invoices, and redirects a payment to a fraudulent account.

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Actor
scenarioInputs.content:
* We consider an actor aligned with the OC1 level of operations, as defined by RAND:
* Operations roughly less capable than or comparable to a single individual with some limited professional expertise in information security, spending several days with a total budget of up to $1,000 on the specific operation, and no pre-existing infrastructure or access to the organization. This includes the operations of many hobbyist hackers, as well as more experienced hackers who implement completely untargeted "spray and pray" attacks.
* Some prominent OC1 actors include Onel de Guzman, a 24-year-old computer science student, responsible for the [ILOVEYOU](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ILOVEYOU) worm, or [Michael Calce](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Michael_Calce), who at 15 launched DDoS attacks that took down Yahoo, Amazon, and CNN for over a week. These are atypical actors at the tail of the distribution, while most OC1 actors are less well known, and could include any computer science graduate or high schooler with access to forums and an interest in cybersecurity.
* Some more recent examples include:
  * The [several teenagers](https://www.theguardian.com/technology/2020/jul/31/twitter-hack-arrests-florida-uk-teenagers) who used phishing to hack the profiles of a number of Twitter celebrities in 2020 to promote cryptocurrency scams (whose techniques were noted as "extremely sloppy").
  * An Australian [13 year old who hacked Apple](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2019-05-27/adelaide-teenager-hacked-into-apple-twice-in-two-years/11152492)'s systems twice in 2015 and 2017 in the hopes of gaining an employment offer at the company
  * A UK based [22 year old who launched a DDoS attack](https://www.theregister.com/2021/06/30/bradley_niblock_election_ddos/) against the website of a Labour Party candidate, taking it down days before the 2019 general election
* These examples serve to illustrate the wide range and potential for impact of even the most amateur hackers

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Target
scenarioInputs.content:
* We consider the target to be a financially attractive or data-rich organisation (Financial Services, Healthcare & Public Health, Commercial Facilities, Information Technology, Cloud Services).
* OC1 actors typically do not have the resources to go after heightened security organisations, such as large banks. For this specific scenario, we will therefore consider the target to be in the range of an SME / Regional Bank / Fintech firm.

scenarioInputs.heading: Type of Vector
scenarioInputs.content:
* The main vector of this scenario is phishing and invoice fraud
* An actor will typically first perform reconnaissance by searching for contacts of employees on social media and other open source intelligence platforms
* The actor will then fake an identity in a communication to gain access to the target's organisation via a malicious link or attachment, generally sending out a high volume of phishing emails
* Once inside the target's systems, the actor monitors the phished employee's inbox, waits for an upcoming transaction, and then requests that the banking details for the transaction be changed to a fraudulent account.

scenarioInputs.heading: Defense Level
scenarioInputs.content:
* Medium-sized organizations such as SMEs or Fintech firms would be expected to have several defenses against phishing attacks. These would typically include:
  * Basic spam filters
  * Partial DMARC/DKIM/SPF enforcement ([20% adoption across domains in 2024](https://www.valimail.com/blog/dmarc-growth-data/), with many adopters setting the lowest level of protection)
  * MFA on some services (email, VPN, banking - [global adoption of MFA in SMBs is 35%](https://cyberreadinessinstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/CRI-Global-MFA-Survey-Slides-2024.pdf))
  * Basic phishing awareness training ([59% of cybersecurity leaders deliver weekly or monthly security awareness training](https://www.egress.com/blog/phishing/phishing-statistics-round-up?utm_source=chatgpt.com), less than [25% of small businesses conduct regular cyber training](https://qualysec.com/small-business-cyber-attack-statistics/#:~:text=19.%2092,passwords%20to%20gain%20unauthorized%20access))
  * The safety culture at these organizations is not always strictly enforced, and employees may not strictly adhere to training and security procedures ([91% of cybersecurity leaders express doubt in the effectiveness of their security awareness training](https://www.egress.com/blog/phishing/phishing-statistics-round-up?utm_source=chatgpt.com), [30% of IT professionals say most of their employees complete the training](https://www.helpnetsecurity.com/2021/07/23/risk-phishing-attacks/))

scenarioInputs.heading: Intent
scenarioInputs.content:
* The intent of the attack is direct monetization. Within this goal there are three main options:
  * Simplified Business Email Compromise (BEC) type attack whereby the actor simply modifies invoice information
  * Data exfiltration/extortion whereby the actor downloads the customers data and resells it or threaten to resell it if they are not provided with a small payment
  * Credentials sale, whereby the actor directly sells on the compromised credentials, typically to a higher tier actor
* This scenario will focus on the first monetization method: the actor modifies an invoice, wire transfer details, or sends a fake payment request. These are not targeted or highly social engineered, and simply attempt to pass in the flow of regular business transactions.
* The second monetization method verges on OC2 operational scale, as it typically requires persistent access for longer than a few days, and may require some form of negotiation, which OC1 actors do not typically engage in. Credential sale does not capture the end-to-end attack, as these credentials are typically used for further operations, and on its own is not such a major financial impact.

attackSteps.description: The actor will typically look for public Open Source Intelligence (OSINT), scraping sites such as Linkedin for relevant role titles, and may look through breached data dumps. The actor may fail at this stage for a given company and still succeed for another, with little cost. Therefore we define this stage as "the actor can find a large list of employee contacts for any organisation that they can reasonably attack".
attackSteps.failureMode: Failure at this stage means that the actor is not able to collect the contact details for sufficient contacts to continue the attack, which could be caused by most organisations having minimal public employee information or non standard/non guessable email formats.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: High probability of success - not worth breaking down for further granularity, all failure modes of different techniques are correlated, an LLM would not uplift different techniques differentially

attackSteps.description: The actor registers a look alike domain (eg. paypal.com vs pay-pal.com) and sets up a basic phishing website, often using a pre-made phishing kit. The aim of this stage is purely to have all the infrastructure required to perform the attack.
attackSteps.failureMode: Domain registrars block the purchase, the phishing kit is a known variant that is detectable by modern security tools, the phishing kit is non-functional, or the actor lacks the technical skill to deploy it on their server/VPS.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: There are not many different methods to be accounted for at this stage. The actor may choose to develop the infrastructure themselves, rather than purchasing a phishing kit, though this would simply reduce their likelihood of success. We can estimate the overall success of acquiring some usable infrastructure at the tactic level.

attackSteps.description: The actor crafts a phishing email, and then sends lures at scale to the targets gathered during reconnaissance. The aim of this stage is purely to get emails through email security into the target's inbox.
attackSteps.failureMode: Failure at this stage means that the email does not land in the majority of inboxes. This could be caused by spam or other filters triggering on the email body itself, the domain being blocked by the target's DMARC policy, or the link being flagged as suspicious by the email gateway's URL scanner.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: The actor may choose variants of this method such as SMS phishing, phishing via attachment or via link, but the likelihoods of success are not substantially different, the failure modes are heavily correlated, and the different modes do not benefit from LLMs differently

attackSteps.description: One of the targets reads the message and is convinced to click on the link, and enter their credentials in the fake website, which are transmitted to the actor. The objective of this stage is purely for the actor to gain access to a set of credentials for the target organisation.
attackSteps.failureMode: Failure at this stage means that no target clicks the link and enters their credentials. This could be caused by the email body or address being obviously fake, or the website being too visually different from the target's system website. Phishing training may reduce the likelihood of any target clicking a link or entering credentials.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: Similarly as above, the actor may choose slight variants of initial access methods, but the failure modes of each are heavily correlated, and there is little differential LLM uplift

attackSteps.description: The actor, using the stolen credentials, successfully logs in and creates a persistence mechanism (e.g., forwarding rule) without being immediately detected and locked out. If the target has multi-factor authentication, the actor may try simple techniques such as fatigue attacks (spamming the authentication request until approved by the target). The actor must be able to survive beyond the first login, and log back in to the account over the next few hours/days to monitor the inbox and identify a suitable payment to act on.
attackSteps.failureMode: Failure at this stage could be caused by the actor not being able to satisfy MFA, or automated systems detecting anomalous login (impossible travel, new login location etc.) terminating the session or triggering a password reset. Success at this stage means that the actor's foothold remains valid beyond the initial session. This entails that they have created mailbox forward rules to survive resets, added accounts, backup access methods, or granted permissions to allow for persistent access.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: At this level, persistence does not have many different sub-techniques that are relevant.
techniques: T1098.002 - Account Manipulation: Additional Email Delegate Permissions

attackSteps.description: Some actors may attempt to phish higher level employees once they have an initial access, but this is not required, and the default scenario is that the actor simply uses their initial access point.
Any failure at this stage would be accounted for in steps such as initial access. There are no specific defence evasion techniques that are not accounted for.
Credential access is already accounted for in other steps.
The actor, while maintaining access, finds a viable, active transaction to intercept within the user's mailbox.
attackSteps.failureMode: All high-value payment communications occur on a separate, secure platform (e.g., a dedicated procurement portal) that the compromised email account has no access to. The actor is detected and their access is revoked before any suitable invoice or payment conversation takes place.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: While the actor might use specific techniques, their action is functionally reading emails and looking for keywords. The failure mode is not finding information in time, not the failure of a specific technical command.

attackSteps.description: The actor can perform all actions required from the target mailbox.
The only command and control required is monitoring incoming emails.
No data is exfiltrated other than forwarded emails.
The actor, having identified a target payment, modifies the banking details to redirect the payment to a fraudulent account. The payment request is approved and processed.
attackSteps.failureMode: A mandatory out-of-band verification policy (e.g., a phone call to a known contact) is triggered by the request to change bank details, which exposes the fraud. The employee responsible for payment notices the discrepancy or the suspicious nature of the request and escalates it.
attackSteps.breakdownRationale: The actor must both send a request for information to be changed without being flagged (which is predominantly social engineering), and the payment must pass without raising automatic checks that would prevent success (mainly related to defences in place).
techniques: T1565.001 - Data Manipulation: Stored Data Manipulation

nodes.name: Reconnaissance
nodes.baselineRationale: The actor searches public data such as LinkedIn for relevant role titles and finds a large number of relevant contacts that are likely to handle financial operations (executives, sales, management etc.). Few organisations conceal staff contact info sufficiently to thwart an amateur's recon. Even if one chosen target has little public data or uses obscure email formats, an OC1 actor can simply pivot to another similar target with minimal effort or cost. It is likely that almost every phishing campaign finds enough employee data to proceed.
nodes.sotaRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: Not elicited due to high baseline probability.

nodes.name: Resource Development
nodes.baselineRationale: The phishing website purchased or developed by the actor passes standard URL checking and spam filters. Registering look-alike domains has been noted as "[inexpensive and easy to execute](https://www.linkedin.com/pulse/anatomy-business-email-compromise-bec-eric-vanderburg/)". [77% of domains](https://www.forbes.com/councils/forbestechcouncil/2024/10/25/newly-registered-domains-to-block-or-not-to-block/) used for phishing were registered for that purpose, and there are approximately [1 million unique phishing sites](https://www.statista.com/statistics/266155/number-of-phishing-attacks-worldwide/) registered in the world. This suggests that actors face little difficulty acquiring domains and hosting phishing pages. A minority of OC1 actors might fail here due to technical errors. For instance, a poorly configured phishing page might not capture credentials correctly, or a registrar might suspend an obvious brand-impersonation domain.
nodes.sotaRationale: Resource development for phishing is primarily an operational task with an already-high baseline success rate. An LLM at this capability ceiling provides modest value through step-by-step troubleshooting guidance, DNS configuration help, and basic template customisation. The primary bottleneck for OC1 actors — technical setup errors in deploying phishing kits — is partially addressable through LLM-provided procedural instructions. However, the LLM cannot meaningfully address the main failure modes such as domain suspension by registrars, payment fraud detection, and hosting provider takedowns. The domain mismatch between the benchmark's code vulnerability analysis and the operational task of infrastructure deployment further limits the benefit. Pre-made phishing kits already minimise technical barriers, so the LLM's incremental contribution is small — primarily faster iteration on domain selection, reduced simple configuration errors, and basic troubleshooting assistance.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of expert-level exploitation tasks can provide substantial assistance with the technical components of resource development, including domain registration, phishing kit deployment, and troubleshooting. It effectively eliminates knowledge barriers for OC1 actors and reduces technical configuration failures significantly. However, the baseline success rate is already high because phishing kits are specifically designed for ease of use. Critical failure modes — domain registrar restrictions, hosting provider detection, and payment processing issues — remain largely unaddressable by better information alone. The severe domain mismatch between advanced exploitation skills and basic infrastructure deployment limits the benefit to modest improvements in reducing technical errors, yielding a small absolute improvement. External factors like domain suspensions and payment issues create hard ceilings that limit improvement potential regardless of LLM capability.

nodes.name: Initial Access
nodes.baselineRationale: The email crafted by the actor, as well as the address used, pass standard filters for a majority of the targets. [14-18% of DMARC registered domains](https://www.darkreading.com/cybersecurity-operations/time-get-strict-dmarc) have an enforced policy, suggesting that the fake domain would only result in a failure in 15% of cases. [47% of phishing emails](https://www.graphus.ai/blog/nearly-half-of-phishing-emails-escape-traditional-filters/#:~:text=Traditional%20spam%20and%20security%20filters,How%20are%20cybercriminals%20slipping%20through) are not caught by traditional email protection solutions, suggesting that on average, every other phishing email passes through filters.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM provides meaningful assistance primarily through professionalising email content — a critical bottleneck for OC1-level actors. Even at this capability ceiling, the LLM excels at natural language generation, enabling amateur actors to craft more convincing business emails. However, the correlation between the benchmark's code vulnerability analysis and social engineering is weak. The LLM might marginally optimise email formatting and word choice, but cannot overcome fundamental constraints like domain reputation or technical filtering. Most experts agree the improvement is modest — better-crafted emails help a marginal number pass both automated filters and human review, but the baseline already reflects effective phishing techniques with limited room for content-based improvements. The wide uncertainty reflects domain divergence between code analysis and social engineering.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM demonstrating expert-level security research capabilities improves phishing email content quality — a core LLM strength and a major OC1 weakness — providing a modest absolute improvement over the baseline. The systematic analysis skills enable better reconnaissance of target email security configurations and optimisation of bypass strategies. However, this improvement is constrained by substantial domain mismatch between exploitation capability and email deliverability expertise, the OC1 actor's limited implementation ability and budget preventing infrastructure improvements, and the baseline already capturing that basic spam filters fail frequently, making large gains difficult. Several experts note that phishing delivery success is primarily about infrastructure reputation, volume, and persistence — not sophistication — limiting the LLM's impact to template optimisation and minor analytical assistance rather than breakthrough capabilities.

nodes.name: Execution
nodes.baselineRationale: At least one target clicks the link in the email, goes to the login page, and enters their credentials in such a way that allows the actor to access the account. The average click rate on malicious links is [17.8%, rising to 53.2%](https://www.paubox.com/blog/2024-phishing-statistics-latest-figures-and-trends#:~:text=For%20example%2C%20%E2%80%9CIndividuals%20working%20for,to%20do%20so%2C%E2%80%9D%20StationX%20adds) for targeted spearphishing. In one study, [11% of users](https://hoxhunt.com/guide/phishing-trends-report#:~:text=Top%203%20most) clicked a link on a first simulated attack down to 2% on the 14th simulated attack. [One in eight](https://www.infosecinstitute.com/resources/phishing/11-phishing-email-subject-lines-your-employees-need-to-recognize/#:~:text=And%20since%201%20out%20of,365%3A%20Change%20Your%20Password%20Immediately) users share their credentials on fake sites. Other sources find [10% of employees click on phishing links, and 62.3% of those enter their credentials.](https://static.fortra.com/terranova-security/pdfs/report/fta-tn-gone-fishing-tournament-report-rp.pdf) Therefore while the probability of each individual is between 2.5% and 25%, if 10 emails are sent, the likelihood of at least one success rises rapidly, lying between 25% and 94%. For the mean estimate we assume 20% click through, and 40% likelihood of entering credentials, resulting in a 56% success rate for ten emails.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's technical exploitation capability demonstrates competence in web vulnerabilities but has weak correlation with phishing execution success, which is primarily driven by human psychology rather than technical sophistication. The baseline already assumes OC1 actors use available phishing kits effectively. The LLM provides marginal improvements through better phishing page customisation, improved credential capture flow mimicry, and reduced technical red flags — addressing visual credibility which accounts for a meaningful portion of the step's success. However, this step fundamentally depends on human psychology and credential submission decisions, limiting leverage. Several experts note the domain gap between web exploitation and operational security, combined with OC1 actors' limited implementation ability. The improvement represents better execution of known techniques rather than a capability breakthrough, with the wide uncertainty reflecting the inherently stochastic nature of employee credential-sharing behaviour.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of expert-level cryptanalysis provides measurable but limited benefit to the execution phase of credential harvesting. Its primary contribution is technical polish — creating more professional-looking phishing pages with fewer glitches and more convincing authentication flows. This addresses a genuine bottleneck for OC1 actors, whose amateur-looking sites can deter credential entry. However, the execution step's success is primarily determined by human psychology — whether a user who has already clicked will enter credentials. The baseline already assumes functional fake pages using existing phishing kits. Some experts note near-zero correlation between cryptanalysis capability and credential entry rates, while others see moderate improvement through reduced technical red flags. The consensus is that the LLM elevates OC1 actors from amateur-with-basic-tools to amateur-with-professional-tools, providing incremental quality improvement rather than a capability transformation.

nodes.name: Persistence
nodes.baselineRationale: The actor is able to persistently log in to the target's account, bypassing any login rules. The actor has access over a sufficient period of time to identify an upcoming invoice or payment that they can modify and log back in to send the modified invoice. Only approximately [35% of SMBs have MFA enabled](https://cyberreadinessinstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/CRI-Global-MFA-Survey-Slides-2024.pdf), therefore the actor has a 65% likelihood of passing MFA. Incidentally, [99.9% of compromised accounts do not have MFA](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2023/01/26/2023-identity-security-trends-and-solutions-from-microsoft/) enabled, suggesting that it is unlikely for an amateur actor to succeed if the target has MFA. The actor may also be caught by impossible travel restrictions or new location sign-ins, though presence of additional security features may be correlated with presence of MFA.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's demonstrated capability far exceeds the technical requirements for email persistence, which is straightforward credential usage and standard email functionality. However, success is dominated by the MFA barrier — roughly 35% of targets have MFA enabled, which the LLM cannot circumvent. Within the reachable population without MFA, the LLM improves success through better operational security guidance, platform-specific forwarding configuration instructions, and reduced technical errors. Several experts note that establishing email persistence is trivial compared to the benchmark's technical exploitation, and the LLM provides limited value beyond procedural guidance and basic operational suggestions. The primary barriers — MFA presence and automated detection systems — remain hard technical controls that LLM assistance cannot address, constraining the improvement to modest gains in the non-MFA target population.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of graduate-level cryptanalysis provides near-perfect guidance for the trivial task of email forwarding setup, effectively eliminating knowledge barriers for OC1 actors. However, the baseline success rate is primarily constrained by immutable technical barriers — MFA in roughly 35% of accounts, IP-based anomaly detection, and automated lockouts — that the LLM cannot bypass. For the roughly 65% of attempts without MFA, the LLM meaningfully improves execution through precise technical instructions, sophisticated detection avoidance tactics, and effective troubleshooting guidance. The LLM provides maximum value where knowledge was the bottleneck, but the limiting factor remains MFA adoption and detection systems, not actor skill. The experts broadly agree that this represents meaningful but not transformative improvement — fundamental security controls remain the primary defence.

nodes.name: Discovery
nodes.baselineRationale: The actor will typically set forwarding rules, to automatically forward any emails containing keywords such as "invoice" to an external inbox owned by the actor. In an SME, a large number of invoices may be handled by email, without a specific dedicated platform. Within SMEs, [42% use emails](https://www.docuclipper.com/blog/accounts-payable-statistics/) for invoicing, with 86% still entering details manually. Only 9% of organisations have fully automated invoicing. Not all employees have access to invoicing, with a recommended [approximately 10% of accountants to staff headcount](https://envoice.eu/en/blog/how-many-accountants-does-a-company-need/). Other employees may encounter invoices for specific projects, with a lower frequency. The actor has likely targeted organisations and employees with a higher likelihood of interacting with payments. The actor may also have gained multiple points of access. There are few sources to estimate this value, therefore we estimate a 60% success rate as over a long period of time it is likely that most employees will encounter some level of invoice, with high uncertainty bounds.
nodes.sotaRationale: The discovery step requires email content analysis and pattern recognition — dramatically simpler than the benchmark's technical exploitation and a core LLM strength. The LLM provides meaningful assistance through more efficient parsing, better pattern recognition across varied invoice formats, and systematic automated analysis that removes the manual scanning bottleneck for OC1 actors. However, success is primarily constrained by whether suitable invoices exist during the persistence window, not by recognition capability. The baseline already assumes adequate human recognition capability. The LLM improves detection of invoices when present and reduces edge case misses, but cannot create targets that do not exist. The wide uncertainty primarily reflects target email usage patterns and invoice availability rather than actor skill, limiting the LLM's differential impact.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The discovery phase is fundamentally opportunity-limited rather than skill-limited. An LLM capable of advanced cryptanalysis possesses vastly superior pattern recognition and automation capabilities compared to what is needed for email monitoring, and will trivially succeed at the identification component. For basic email monitoring and invoice recognition, the LLM's expert-level capabilities are vastly over-qualified. The primary constraint — whether suitable invoices exist during the monitoring window — remains unchanged. The LLM eliminates most manual review failures and pattern recognition gaps, providing systematic monitoring that reduces human error. However, the wide uncertainty persists because invoice availability dominates technical capability as the variance driver. Several experts emphasise that this step is constrained by environmental factors rather than actor sophistication.

nodes.name: T1565.001 - Stored Data Manipulation
nodes.baselineRationale: Having identified an email chain containing an invoice, the actor will modify the invoice to contain fraudulent account details, and send it to the payer with a request to update details. Over a number of real BEC cases, [28% of received BEC emails were opened, and 15% received replies](https://files.abnormalsecurity.com/production/images/blog/CISO-Guide-BEC.pdf?dm=1731697078). This includes cases where the email was sent via a spoofed rather than a phished address (significantly lowering the likelihood of success, as initial access and execution only have a combined success rate of 25%). We estimate that when coming from a trusted address, the success rates would be significantly higher. Another source found out of [13 attempted invoice frauds, 9 of them succeeded.](https://www.medius.com/media/vqfj0a0b/medius-financial-census-2024.pdf?ref=precoro.com) To be classified as attempted, we assume that the actor arrived at the stage of modifying an invoice's details. This is a 69% success rate. Given the high uncertainty, we therefore estimate a success rate of 45% at this step.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's demonstrated capability at the benchmark indicates moderate specialised knowledge, but invoice modification via BEC is fundamentally a communication task — a domain where LLMs typically exceed their technical exploitation capabilities. For OC1 actors with limited professional expertise, access to professional-grade business writing, persuasive framing, and consistent communication addresses a key bottleneck in social engineering success. The LLM elevates amateur phishing language to professional business correspondence, reducing obvious linguistic red flags. Several experts note this represents meaningful but not transformative assistance — professional communication cannot overcome all organisational verification procedures, employee vigilance, and out-of-band confirmation policies. The persistent ceiling from human scrutiny and organisational controls prevents higher success rates, and the wide uncertainty reflects high variance in social engineering outcomes.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of solving graduate-level cryptanalysis challenges demonstrates exceptional business communication capabilities, and invoice fraud social engineering is well-aligned with core LLM competencies in language, persuasion, and business communication. For an OC1 actor with low expertise, the primary bottleneck — lack of sophistication in communication — is substantially addressed. The LLM produces professional-quality, convincing invoice modifications that avoid common linguistic red flags. However, success is ultimately gated by organisational controls, verification procedures, and strategic factors beyond message quality. Several experts estimate significant absolute improvement, reflecting that many baseline failures were due to poor communication quality which the LLM directly addresses. The remaining failures occur due to human vigilance and procedural controls that AI assistance cannot bypass, regardless of sophistication.

nodes.name: T1657 - Financial Theft
nodes.baselineRationale: Once the details have been changed, the funds are transferred to an account owned by the actor. At this stage, automatic checks can prevent the transfer, although these may be rare in SMEs, and law enforcement may succeed in recovering some of the funds. In 2022, the FBI Recovery Asset Team was able to recover [74% of US domestic fund transfers, and only 14% of international transfers](https://www.welivesecurity.com/2022/04/26/trouble-bec-how-stop-costliest-scam/). It is likely that the actor has an overseas account to render recovery more difficult. Other sources cite a [25% partial recovery rate.](https://www.insurancejournal.com/news/national/2025/05/12/823055.htm)
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's demonstrated capability in structured technical exploitation has minimal relevance to this financial theft step, which depends on banking fraud detection systems and law enforcement recovery capabilities rather than technical skill. The baseline already assumes actors use international accounts — the primary strategy an LLM would suggest — leaving little room for meaningful assistance. Experts broadly agree that this step is largely passive once the fraudulent payment is initiated, with success determined by automated banking controls and institutional recovery procedures outside the actor's real-time control. The LLM could provide marginal tactical advice on withdrawal timing or jurisdiction selection, but the OC1 actor's limited expertise and budget constrain implementation of sophisticated financial strategies. The wide uncertainty largely reflects variability in banking and law enforcement processes rather than uncertainty about LLM impact.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM's demonstrated capability in advanced cryptanalysis has minimal application to this attack step. By the financial theft stage, success depends primarily on banking fraud detection systems, law enforcement recovery capabilities, and jurisdictional factors — not technical exploitation skills. The baseline already assumes actors use optimal strategies such as international accounts. The LLM could provide marginal operational improvements through better extraction timing, communication strategy, and jurisdiction selection, but cannot influence the primary determinants of success: automated banking controls and institutional recovery procedures. Experts broadly agree this is fundamentally a passive step where the actor cannot directly control the outcome, and technical sophistication in one domain provides minimal advantage in the fundamentally different domain of financial infrastructure and law enforcement procedures.

nodes.name: Number of Actors
nodes.baselineRationale: APWG observed [32k phishing campaigns per month](https://docs.apwg.org/reports/apwg_trends_report_q2_2024.pdf) in Q2 2024, with 10% targeting financial institutions. A given phishing platform has an estimated [10k users](https://thehackernews.com/2024/04/global-police-operation-disrupts.html), of which some distribution are OC1 and OC2 actors. [SilverTerrier](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/SilverTerrier) is a collection of over 400 unique actors performing BEC, located in Nigeria alone. It is understood that many of these actors began as novices, but have recently started to coordinate further. A given phishing kit store, W3ll Store, was frequented by over [500 actors](https://duo.com/decipher/w3ll-threat-actor-built-a-microsoft-365-phishing-ecosystem#:~:text=The%20group%2C%20called%20W3LL%2C%20has,at%20least%20500%20threat%20actors). A [2024 takedown](https://www.interpol.int/en/News-and-Events/News/2024/Major-cybercrime-operation-nets-1-006-suspects#:~:text=Operation%20Serengeti%20%282%20September%20,Africa%20Cyber%20Threat%20Assessment%20Report) of African BEC, ransomware and extortion actors arrested 1,006 individuals. While there is likely significant overlap between groups and a mix of different levels of actors, it is reasonable to estimate from these numbers that there are several thousands of amateur actors motivated to perform BEC.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM's capability ceiling far exceeds BEC technical requirements. However, BEC already has low technical barriers — the primary constraint is criminal motivation and intent, which LLMs cannot create. The LLM's main impact is removing language barriers and building confidence among marginal actors who already possessed criminal intent, yielding a modest population increase rather than a dramatic shift. Several experts note that the dominant limiting factors — psychological barriers, willingness to commit fraud, financial infrastructure for money laundering, and legal risk — remain entirely unchanged by LLM assistance. One dissenting view estimates a larger uplift of around 40% through reducing technical barriers for infrastructure setup, reconnaissance, and basic scripting, but the majority view is that non-technical barriers dominate the actor pool size.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of solving advanced cryptanalysis challenges is vastly overqualified for basic phishing and BEC operations. Technical and knowledge barriers, which currently filter a significant portion of potentially motivated actors, are substantially reduced by LLM assistance. However, critical limiting factors remain unchanged: legal risk, financial infrastructure requirements, ethical barriers, and the fundamental need for criminal motivation. Experts disagree on the magnitude of the increase — estimates range from a modest 15-20% increase (arguing technical barriers were never the primary constraint) to roughly doubling the actor population (arguing the LLM dramatically lowers the skill floor). The consensus is that the LLM primarily enables criminally-motivated but technically-limited individuals who had specific implementation knowledge gaps, rather than fundamentally transforming the threat landscape. Existing defensive controls remain effective against OC1-level actors even with LLM assistance.

nodes.name: Number of Attempts per Actor
nodes.baselineRationale: Phishing kits cost between [$50 and $250/month](https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/caffeine-phishing-service-platform), rendering it unlikely that an OC1 actor with a budget of $1,000 is active for more than several months in a year. With an active subscription, actors may at least perform one operation per month. If each campaign takes several days to a week to complete, it is feasible to expect an upper bound of around 100 per year for active actors. Failure at an early stage of the attack costs little to the attacker, and therefore a much larger number of attacks could be attempted, potentially up to one per day. A given kit, CoGUI Phish, was used for up to [50 different campaigns](https://www.proofpoint.com/uk/blog/threat-insight/cogui-phish-kit-targets-japan-millions-messages) in a month. SilverTerrier, with an assumed 500 actors, performed [90,000](https://cyberscoop.com/silverterrier-email-scam-nigeria/#:~:text=enforcement%20and%20prominent%20figures%20from,%E2%80%9D) attacks in a month. It is therefore likely that the mean number of attempts for an OC1 actor lies between 3 (one attack per quarter) and 100 (multiple times per week).
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM accelerates phishing content generation, providing roughly 30-50% time savings on the content task, and reduces knowledge barriers through professional-quality output. This translates to a modest increase in attempt frequency. However, operational constraints — the $1,000 budget, detection risks, and operational security concerns — remain constant and cap the upside potential. Most experts agree the improvement is in the range of 10-25% more attempts annually, primarily through faster email content generation and basic automation scripting. The core bottlenecks of budget limitations, single-actor operational capacity, and detection risk that scales with volume remain unchanged. One expert notes that OC1 actors already use phishing kit templates, so the marginal value over existing automation is modest — primarily better customisation and quality, not dramatic efficiency gains.
nodes.saturatedRationale: An LLM capable of solving complex exploitation tasks provides meaningful but constrained efficiency gains for phishing and BEC campaigns. The primary benefit is automated email generation (reducing days to hours per campaign) and faster infrastructure setup. However, experts disagree on the magnitude: one estimates a 1.7x increase from baseline reflecting substantial automation gains, while others see only modest 4-25% improvements, arguing the core bottlenecks — budget constraints, operational capacity of a single individual, and cash-out logistics — remain binding regardless of LLM capability. The upper bound is constrained by operational realities: even with perfect automation, manual monitoring of campaigns and invoice manipulation limits scale. The consensus is that the LLM provides real time savings in setup phases but does not fundamentally change the attack tempo, which is limited more by financial resources and human operational bandwidth than by content generation speed.

nodes.name: Direct Cost of Invoice Payment
nodes.baselineRationale: Small Australian businesses lose an average of [$15,500 to $23,370 to fake invoices](https://eftsure.com/statistics/invoice-fraud-statistics/#source-wrapper). The FBI's 2022 crime report stated [$2.7Bn losses from BEC over 21,832 complaints](https://www.ic3.gov/AnnualReport/Reports/2022_IC3Report.pdf), an average of $130k per loss. Note that this applies also to much larger (OC2 and OC3) cases of BEC, with damages going up to [£18.5M from one attack](https://www.actionfraud.police.uk/alert/action-fraud-warning-after-serious-rise-in-ceo-fraud#:~:text=The%20largest%20reported%20amount%20of,%C2%A335%2C000%20but%20this%20can%20vary). Only [11% of losses from BEC are below $10k](https://www.bitdefender.com/en-us/blog/hotforsecurity/a-single-phishing-email-can-cost-a-small-business-100k-heres-how-to-protect-your-office-against-fraud), with 37% between $50k-100k. 33% of BEC attacks target small businesses, costing an average of [$50k per attack](https://qualysec.com/small-business-cyber-attack-statistics/#:~:text=19.%2092,passwords%20to%20gain%20unauthorized%20access). Medius' 2024 financial census reported invoice fraud to average [$130k in the US](https://www.medius.com/media/vqfj0a0b/medius-financial-census-2024.pdf?ref=precoro.com). In 2015-2016 the average successful UK CEO Fraud (BEC impersonating a CEO) took [£35k](https://www.actionfraud.police.uk/alert/action-fraud-warning-after-serious-rise-in-ceo-fraud#:~:text=The%20largest%20reported%20amount%20of,%C2%A335%2C000%20but%20this%20can%20vary) (= £50k), though this is a more advanced form of BEC.
nodes.sotaRationale: The LLM at this capability level provides modest assistance for BEC attacks primarily through improved targeting and systematic execution. The main impact mechanism is improved reconnaissance enabling better targeting toward higher-value SMEs and superior invoice selection within compromised accounts. However, damage remains fundamentally constrained by SME transaction sizes and existing business relationships — the LLM enhances target selection and execution quality but does not unlock new attack vectors or overcome inherent business limits on invoice amounts. Experts estimate the uplift ranges from roughly 6% to 26% above baseline, reflecting optimised decision-making within existing attack constraints. The primary uncertainty stems from victim organisation characteristics rather than LLM capability.
nodes.saturatedRationale: The LLM's strong analytical capabilities provide modest but meaningful improvements in BEC attack damage through better invoice discovery, target selection, and communication quality. The primary mechanisms are systematic identification of higher-value invoices and improved execution quality. However, impact is fundamentally bounded by available invoice values in compromised SME accounts. Several experts note severe domain mismatch between the benchmark's cryptanalysis capability and BEC social engineering requirements. Estimates of improvement range from roughly 7% to 64% above baseline, with the higher estimates reflecting sophisticated analytical capabilities transferring to invoice targeting and the lower estimates reflecting the environmental ceiling of SME payment patterns. The consensus is that the LLM enhances operational decision-making within existing constraints but cannot overcome the structural limits of SME transaction sizes and detection thresholds.
